figures.caption: Figure 2: (a) Standard IV model with unmeasured confounding U ; (b) proposed model, where U captures dependence arising from using the same outcome GWAS data to estimate γ̂Ou j and Γ̂Ou j .

Table 1: Simulation results for various estimators under Scenarios (iii) and (iv). Reported summary statistics include the bias, root-mean-square error (RMSE), confidence interval (CI) length, and CI coverage rate (nominal level 95 %). We report (Bias/β0) × 100%, (RMSE/β0) × 100%, (CI length/β0) × 100%, and CI coverage rate. Cell colors indicate performance, with yellow denoting poor and green denoting favorable results.

Figure 5: Estimated causal effect of BMI on HDL using different thresholds and estimators. Treatment data are shown for South Asian (purple), Latin American (blue), Japanese (red), and African (black) populations.
Table 4: Comparison of results in the BMI-HDL example with heterogeneous treatment GWAS sources. Reported values are point estimates, with confidence intervals shown in brackets
Table 3: P-values from heterogeneity tests comparing SNP-BMI associations between European and non-European ancestries
Figure 3: Comparison of MR models with and without Pleiotropy effects.

Table 2: Simulation results for various estimators under Scenarios (iii) and (iv). Reported summary statistics include the bias, root-mean-square error (RMSE), confidence interval (CI) length, and CI coverage rate (nominal level 95 %). We report (Bias/β0) × 100%, (RMSE/β0) × 100%, (CI length/β0) × 100%, and CI coverage rate. Cell colors indicate performance, with yellow denoting poor and green denoting favorable results.
